advancedsteelmaking-text:
Advanced Steelmaking
One step for a seraph, a thousand plates for all of humanity.

Steelmaking Expanded adds a full industrial pipeline that works in two stages.Stage I stands up a blast furnace and itssupport machinery to smelt iron in bulk. Stage II bolts aBessemer converter onto that line to refine the molten iron intosteel. Build the iron stage first and get it running reliably — the steel stage isexpensive and feeds directly off it.
To construct most of these structures you need to have tier 3 refractory bricks,which means you need to achieve steel age before this!


════ STAGE I — IRON PRODUCTION ════

1. The Blast Furnace
The blast furnace is theheart of the operation: a tall multiblock built mostly from refractory bricks,with an iron door, two tuyeres (airintakes) low on the shaft and two gas outlets high up. Place the door block andshift + rightmouse it to see the build outline, then fill inevery highlighted block until the structure reports complete.

The furnace does not eat ore directly; it burns blast mix, a packed pile of ore and fuel. You never craft blastmix by hand — it is produced for you by a pair of hoppers stacked abovethe shaft. The ReinforcedHopper is the magazine: open it (rightmouse) and load three things —crushed iron ore, crushed coke, andlime (flux). Crush iron ore and coke in a helve hammer or byhand; coke itself is baked from coal in a coke oven. The hopper combines 12 crushed iron + 3 crushed coke + 1lime into 16 blast mix at a time and stockpiles it internally.

Nothing is wasted if you fumble a pile. Blast mix behaves just like acoal pile: should you ever end up holding loose blast mix — knocking a pile out ofthe shaft, breaking a hopper, scooping spillage off the floor — simplyrightmouse the ground to lay it back down and stack more onto it, thenfeed it back into the furnace. It also still burns as a fuel in a pinch, so astray stack is never lost.

Below it sits the Bell Hopper,which drips that stockpiled blast mix down into the furnace shaft. Toggledropping on or off with ctrl + rightmouse on the reinforcedhopper; it stops on its own once the furnace is full. So loading the furnace isreally just keeping the reinforced hopper supplied with crushed ore, coke andlime — the hoppers handle the rest.

To fire it, let the bell hopper fill the furnace, ignite the blastmix inside, and close the door. Once every pile in thehearth is burning, the furnace is full, the door is shut and the exhaust outletsare not choked, it will begin firing. Internal temperature climbs toward about1400 °C on its own, so we will help the furnace to rise to higher temps in thenext step. Hold it above iron's melting point (~1482 °C) for a fewminutes and the furnace enters its Melting phase, steadilyconverting blast mix into a reservoir of molten iron andmolten slag.

The fire is fragile. Opening the door, running out of blast mix, a choked orbackfed exhaust, or letting the molten reservoir overflow will all snuff it out —keep the exhaust flowing and the door shut while it works.

2. The Cowper Stove — hot blast
A raw blast furnacetops out around 1400 °C, which is not enough. To push past that you reclaim theheat it throws away. A CowperStove is a regenerator: pipe the furnace's Exhaust into itand it soaks that heat into its brick core ("charging"); burning a coal pilebeneath it charges it faster, with anthracite fastest. Be ware, that burning lowgrades of coal results in production of exhaust from the cowper stove mainoutput!
Once charged, route plain Blast (air underpressure) through the same stove and it pours back out scorching hot. Feed thatblast into the furnace tuyeres and the internal temperature jumps toward ~1700°C, melting iron far faster.

The catch: a single stove can only do one job at a time, and a charged coreruns down as you draw blast from it. The intended setup is therefore twocowper stoves working as a pair, cycling between themselves — while onecharges on exhaust, the other discharges hotblast into the furnace; when the discharging one cools, swap their roles (withvalves) so the freshly charged stove takes over. Alternating like this keeps asteady stream of hot blast on the tuyeres without ever stalling. Keep exhaust andair on their separate paths — if they mix inside a stove it cannot heat.

3. The Gas Network
Everything above breathes through a network of gas pipes. Gas comes in three kinds: ordinary Air,hot Exhaust spilling from the furnace and converter, and forcedBlast (pressurised air). Straight pipes, bends, T- andX-junctions carry it, and a few special blocks shape the flow:
• Intakes draw fresh Air into thenetwork from the open world.
• Blowers are powered by an axleand pressurise Air into Blast — this is what forces gas throughthe cowper stove and into the tuyeres.
• Valves open and close a line byhand, letting you redirect flow — essential for swapping the two cowper stovesbetween charge and discharge.
• Pressure valves only openonce the gas behind them builds enough pressure, regulating a line automatically.
• Outlets connect the network to astructure's face (a furnace tuyere, a stove, the converter intake).
Each network tracks its gas type, volume and temperature; an open-ended pipeleaks.

4. The Smoke Stack
A blast furnace produces more exhaust than the cowper stoves can swallow,especially when only one is charging. The Smoke Stack vents the surplus to the sky, keeping the gas networkfrom backing up — a backed-up exhaust line chokes and extinguishes the furnace,so treat the smoke stack as a safety valve for the whole system.

5. Molten Canals
Liquid metal is plumbed, not carried. Lay a Molten Canal (Start) as the anchor of a network, then run straights, bends andjunctions from it. Drain the furnace with its Molten Metal Taps — the lower tap pours iron, the upper tap poursslag — each into the start of a canal below it; toggle a tap withrightmouse. A Canal Tapat the far end pours the metal back out.

Sealing — a manual valve. A straight canal can be turned into a shut-off. rightmouse an empty straight with 4 fire clay and it caps over on both ends, severing the network at that block — flow stops there and the two sides become independent runs, so you can isolate a section without tearing it up. You can only seal a canal that has been drained first; a canal with metal still in it refuses. To reopen it, rightmouse the sealed block with a chisel to chip the plug out and recover 2 fire clay.

Mind the temperature. Metal sitting in the canals slowlycools, and if it drops too far it solidifies in place — ahardened canal stops flowing and must be broken to clear it (you recover most ofthe metal as bits). Keep runs short, keep them fed, and empty them before theyor the furnace go cold.

A canal tap can pour into two destinations. A Mold Pedestal casts the metal into shape: place a fired mold on itand it fills from the network. This mod adds larger casting molds —plate, quad rod and double ingot— that you clay-form and fire like any tool mold. A freshly filled mold isdangerously hot: holding one bare-handed will burn you (wear heavy leather orblacksmith's gloves), and a still-molten mold will spill if you pick it up orrack it before it sets.

A Molten Barrel is the otherdestination — bulk storage for liquid metal. Use it as a buffer forexcess iron the molds can't take fast enough, or to holdslag drained off the top tap. Slag is not waste — solidifiedslag grinds into powdered slag, which has two uses: mixed withslaked lime in a barrel it yields mortar (the powder reacts withthe lime to set, so it stands in for the sand in an ordinary mortar mix), andspread on farmland it acts as a phosphate fertilizer with alasting soil boost — a tidy way to turn a furnace byproduct into building stockand crop yield.

Operating the furnace — the short version
Once everything is built and piped, a production run goes:
1. Stock the reinforced hopper with crushed iron ore,crushed coke and lime, and enable dropping on the bell hopper.
2. Let the shaft fill with blast mix, ignite it, andclose the door.
3. Run a blower (powered by an axle) to push Air through acharged cowper stove and into the tuyeres as hot blast; ventspare exhaust through the smoke stack.
4. Watch the temperature climb past ~1482 °C into the Meltingphase; alternate your two cowper stoves so the blast never goes cold.
5. When iron and slag have pooled, open the molten metal tapsto drain them down the canals into molds (for ingots/plates) orbarrels (for storage and slag) — before anything solidifies.
6. Keep the hopper supplied, the exhaust flowing and repeat.


════ STAGE II — STEEL PRODUCTION ════

The Bessemer Converter
The Bessemer Convertertakes the molten iron your furnace produces and blows it into steel.It is the most demanding machine in the mod — it costs a lot of materials, drawsboth mechanical power and a Blast gas supply, and only works as the final stage ofan already-running molten line. Do not attempt it until your iron tier isestablished and reliably filling canals; the converter is fed by thatline, not a replacement for it.

Building it. The converter is a 3×3×3 vessel raised throughright-click construction rather than placed in one piece. Place itscontrol block first, then rightmouse it with 4rusty gears and 12 iron or steel rods in your hotbar to start the vessel,and build up the construction stages until it reports complete. Around the vesselsit its service ports: a gas intake, a mechanicaltransmission, an input tap, and an output.

Plugging it into the line. The converter needs two serviceswired in from the systems you already built in Tier I:
• Mechanical power — connect an axle to the converter'stransmission so the vessel can tilt and blow.
• Blast — pipe a Blast gas supply (from a blower, the samekind that feeds the furnace) into its gas intake. The intakemust face the same way as the control block or it won't couple.
Then run a molten canal from a furnace tap into the converter'sinput tap, and a second canal off its output toward your molds.

Operating it. With power and blast connected:
1. Set the converter to Filling and pour molten iron into itsinput tap from the canal until charged.
2. Switch to Normal. With power and blast flowing, it blowsair through the charge at ~1600 °C for about five minutes, refining the iron intosteel.
3. Set it to Pouring to drain the finished steel into theoutput canal.
From there the steel travels the molten network exactly like iron — pour itthrough canals into mold pedestals to cast plates, rods andingots, or into a barrel to bank it.

The same temperature rule applies inside the vessel: if the charge ever coolsand solidifies before you pour it, break the converter with asteel pickaxe to recover the metal, then rebuild and try again. Keep the blast onand the charge moving, and the Bessemer will turn out steel as fast as your ironline can feed it.
blockdesc-blastfurnacedoor*: The iron door of the blast furnace.
blockdesc-hopperreinforced*: Combines crushed ores into blast mix for the bell hopper below.
blockdesc-hopperbell*: Drips stockpiled blast mix down into the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnace-tuyere*: A hot blast intake for the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnacetap*: Drains the furnace's molten reservoir into a canal below.
blockdesc-slag*: A block of solidified slag from the blast furnace.
blockdesc-solidifiediron*: Iron that cooled and hardened before it could be tapped or poured.
blockdesc-cowperstove-intake*: The intake face of a cowper stove.
blockdesc-cowperstoveheatsink*: The core of the cowper stove that stores reclaimed exhaust heat.
blockdesc-smokestack-intake*: Vents surplus furnace exhaust to the sky.
blockdesc-gaspipe-straight*: A straight length of gas pipe.
blockdesc-gaspipe-bend*: A 90° bend in a gas pipe run.
blockdesc-gaspipe-heated*: A basic heater that can raise the temprerature of passing air.
blockdesc-gaspipe-passthrough*: Routes a straight pipe through a solid block.
blockdesc-gaspipe-valve*: A hand-operated valve that opens or closes a gas line.
blockdesc-gaspipe-pressurevalve*: Opens once there is enough gas pressure behind it.
blockdesc-gaspipe-blower*: Axle-powered basic blower for pressuring air into blast.
blockdesc-gaspipe-intake*: Draws fresh air into the gas network from outside.
blockdesc-gaspipe-outlet*: Allows a sturcture to expell gas into the network.
blockdesc-moltencanal-start*: The anchor of a molten canal network.
blockdesc-moltencanal-bend*: A 90° bend in a molten canal run.
blockdesc-moltencanal-tap*: Pours metal out of the far end of a canal.
blockdesc-moltencanal-moldpedestal*: Holds a fired mold and fills it from the canal network.
blockdesc-moltenbarrel*: Bulk storage for liquid metal or slag.
blockdesc-bessemercontrol*: Used to operate the bessemer converter.
blockdesc-bessemerconverter*: Massive vessel that blows molten iron into steel.
blockdesc-bessemertransmission*: Couples an axle's mechanical power to the converter so the vessel can tilt and blow.
itemdesc-slag*: A by-product tapped from the blast furnace.
itemdesc-powderedslag*: Ground slag, future mortar or fertilizer.
blockhelp-bessemer-spawn: Place converter vessel (needs 4 rusty gears + 12 iron/steel rods)
bessemer-err-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
bessemer-err-notbuilt: The converter vessel is not finished being built.
bessemer-err-nopower: No mechanical power — connect an axle to the transmission.
bessemer-err-intake-misaligned: The gas intake must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-info-intake-misaligned: Gas intake misaligned — it must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-err-transmission-misaligned: The transmission must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-info-transmission-misaligned: Transmission misaligned — it must face the same way as the control block.
bessemer-err-converter-present: The converter vessel is already in place.
bessemer-err-converter-blocked: The converter cannot be placed — clear the 3×3×3 space first.
bessemer-err-materials: Needs {0} rusty gears and {1} iron/steel rods in your hotbar.
bf-info-doorclosed: Door must be closed for firing!
bf-info-exhaustfull: Exhaust network is full! Production halted.
bf-info-needsmix: Blast Furnace needs more blast mix to fire!
bf-info-partiallylit: Piles are partially lit. Waiting for the fire to spread...
bf-info-ready: Blast Furnace is ready! Ignite the blast mix inside and close the door.
bf-error-incomplete: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
structure-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
tap-err-nocanal: No canal start found below the tap!
bessemer-status-solidified: Solidified — break the converter to clear it
bessemer-status-filling-nometal: Filling — no metal in input tap
cowperstove-status-exhaustmix: Cannot heat up, exhaust mixes with blast!
canal-solidified: Canal solidified! Break and replace all blocks in the network.
canal-err-moldtoosmall: This mold is too small for the tap — use a mold pedestal.
canal-err-sealnotempty: Cannot seal a canal that still holds molten metal — drain it first.
moldpedestal-err-moldtoolarge: This mold will not fit on the pedestal.
moltenspill-error: The molten metal spilled out of the mold!
